text: The study area is located in the nearshore sea area of Jidong Nanpu, adjacent to Caofeidian on the east side. It belongs to the tidal flat area and is the main oil production area of Jidong Nanpu Oilfield. It is also the main distribution area for the construction of artificial islands and oil production platforms in Jidong Nanpu Oilfield. Therefore, studying the shallow strata conditions in this area, analyzing the stratigraphic sequence, and understanding the disaster geological factors within this region are of great practical significance for the development and construction of oil fields. This paper utilizes a large amount of shallow stratigraphic profile data from the Nanpu Sea area conducted by the Exploration and Development Research Institute of Jidong Oilfield in 2007, combined with previous work, to study the shallow sedimentary system in this area, divide the shallow seismic sequence, and analyze the disaster geological factors in this area, with the aim of providing a scientific basis for engineering construction and other aspects in this region. This aspect is also included in one of my papers With a relatively detailed elaboration [1]. The tidal dynamic geomorphic features of the Nanpu Sea area in the Bohai Bay are rather complex. The study of the tidal current dynamic geomorphic features of this sea area will be of great significance to the construction of artificial islands and other projects in this sea area. The author utilized the measured tide level, ocean current, side-scan sonar, and bottom sediment sampling data of this sea area to study and analyze the tidal current geomorphic features of the sea area where the three artificial islands of Nanpu Oilfield are located, infer the migration characteristics of the sand dam in the survey area, and study the impact of artificial island construction and other aspects on this area The influence of the trend characteristics. Based on the collection of a large amount of environmental and geological survey data conducted in this area, this paper analyzes the background of the disaster geological environment in the study area from aspects such as hydrometeorology, topography and geomorphology, sedimentary environment, acoustic strata, engineering geology and structure, and earthquake, and identifies various types of disaster geology. At the same time, the scientific meanings of disaster geology and geological disasters, as well as the connections and differences between the two, were discussed, and a systematic summary was made on the current classification methods of disaster geological types. Through systematic analysis of various data in this area, multiple potential geological types of disasters have been identified, including tidal current erosion channels, shallow beaches, buried ancient river channels, and shallow gas, etc. From the perspectives of formation mechanism, distribution characteristics and potential harmfulness, these types were analyzed in detail.

Laolonggou is a typical sanddam-lagoon-type tidal channel with a complete geomorphic structure of the tidal channel. The tidal current force is the strongest at the entrance of Laolonggou, with a deep trough of -20 meters developed. A complete multi-level waterway system is developed on the tidal current delta inside the entrance. The low tide delta outside the mouth gate ends at a depth of -11 meters. It is marked by a protruding end dam. The main low tide waterway develops on the east side of the low tide delta, while a wide buffer flow platform develops on the west side. The main channel of low tide is dominated by low tide, while the tidal force on the impulse platform is the weakest. The sediment is mainly silty clay. The sediment on the separated sand dam and the end dam is mainly sandy, demonstrating the secondary sorting effect of waves. The topographic comparison from 1945 to 2006 shows the mixed control effect of waves and tidal currents on the geomorphic evolution of Laolonggou. The eastward waves cause the migration of the outer sand dam and the dividing sand dam, forcing the change of the direction of the low tide jet flow and causing the oscillation of the low tide channel. The coastal sediment flow from east to west within the broken wave zone enters the channel from the upper reaches of Laolonggou, and part of it accumulates in the intertidal zone of the lagoon inside the mouth gate. The vast majority of the sediment accumulates in the Laolonggou Low Tide Delta.

The coastline of South China is tortuous and long, with continuous bays, among which the sandy coast stretches for thousands of kilometers. Due to its location in the subtropical and tropical Marine monsoon climate, there is no winter. Spring and autumn last for more than six months, with small temperature changes and pleasant humidity. Many coastal beaches are tourist attractions and are the best places for people to enjoy leisure and entertainment such as sea bathing, sand bathing and sunbathing. However, at present, the damage to these tourism resources is quite severe. Among them, there are natural factors causing damage, such as the erosion of the beach surface caused by storm surges. But more often, it is due to the insufficient understanding of the migration laws of the sand body on the beach, and the direct or indirect human activities leading to the erosion of the beach. The phenomenon of the dynamic balance migration and disintegration of the beach is quite serious. This paper comprehensively studies the dynamic processes of sandy beaches in South China from different perspectives and using different methods, with the aim of providing a theoretical basis for the protection and sustainable development and utilization of sandy beaches in South China. This paper mainly starts from the influence of geological structure on the morphology of sandy beaches in South China, the evolution of the morphology and sediment characteristics of sandy beaches in South China under normal dynamic conditions such as waves and tides, the response mechanism of sandy beaches in South China to typhoon waves and the process of beach reshaping after typhoon waves, the erosion problems and protective measures of sandy beaches in South China, as well as the engineering disasters of sandy beaches, and discusses the sandy beaches in South China Dynamic geomorphic process

The sandbar-lagoon coast accounts for more than 13% of the world's coastline, is composed of three major geomorphic units: sandbars, lagoons and tidal channels, forming an organic whole. It is mainly distributed along the eastern coast of the United States and the Gulf of Mexico, South America and the east coast of India, the Baltic Sea, the Mediterranean Sea, the Caspian Sea and the Black Sea. Youdaoplaceholder0 In China, it is mainly distributed along the coasts of Hebei, Shandong, Guangdong, Hainan and Taiwan provinces. Youdaoplaceholder1 The coast of Jiangsu before the Yellow River took over the Huai River also belongs to the sandbar-lagoon coast. Since this type of coast has great development and utilization value in ports and waterways, aquaculture, environmental ecology, mineral resources, tourist attractions, etc. Youdaoplaceholder0 prompts research on its dynamic and geomorphic processes. International research began in the mid-19th century with studies on the causes of sand dams [1], and reached a research peak from the 1960s to the early 1990s. Systematic theoretical discussions and applied studies on sand dams, lagoons, and tidal channels were conducted [2-5]. Due to the need for port and waterway construction, China began to study the sandbar-lagoon coast in the early 1980s [6-10]. Since the mid-1980s, the research on tidal channels has been relatively advanced [11-14].

Tidal sand ridges are linear sand bodies of tidal formation that develop in nearshore shallow seas, their formation is related to tidal flow velocity, material basis, and land appearance and body location. The tidal sand ridge of Qinzhou Bay is developed at the exit south of the bay neck (Figure 1), sand ridge and deep trough alternate the arrangement, extends in the same direction as the current, forms a 'small' shape and extends southward. It is generally believed that the current flow velocity reaches 1 to 3 knots (that is, tidal sand ridges can be formed in the range of 51.44cm/s to 180.04cm/s [6]). The speed of the rising tide in the eastern ridge and trough area of the outer bay of Qinzhou Bay reaches 1.47 knots (75.8cm/s). The speed of the falling current reaches 1.82 knots (93.7cm/s). The upward trend in the western ridge and trough area has reached 1.53 knots (78.7cm/s), the speed of the falling current reaches 2.57 knots (132.2cm/s). It can be seen that the current velocity in the outer bay of Qinzhou Bay is extremely favorable to the development of sand ridges, thus formed the current dynamic geomorphic feature of alternating tidal sand ridges and deep troughs.

Based on the characteristics of various dynamic geomorphic types mentioned above, borehole sedimentary sequence, and the characteristics of its sedimentary environment changes combined with age determination data and micropaleontological analysis results, it is believed that the Beilun River Estuary area existed before the Holocene as land that had been weathered and eroded, cut by flake flow and river flow, forming the ancient Beilun River channel. The bedrock was exposed, the entire Hekou area was severely invaded by corrosion, the ground was uneven, and it had a natural slope that slightly inclined towards the sea, forming the eroded base of the Jurassic strata. Entering the early Holocene, with glacier melting and sea level rise, seawater flowed into the ancient Beilun River course from the Beibu Gulf. At that time, the Beilun River Estuary was approximately located between present-day Liwei Island of China and Tulagu in Vietnam, in the southeast sea area of the island. Within the river mouth there existed an ancient riverbed, which was deep. A brownish-yellow, purple-red coarse gravel layer and grayish-white, brown, and yellow thin layers of floodplain clay were deposited in the ancient riverbed. The coarse gravel layer of the ancient riverbed in the Hekou area is also found in the Ren River along the coast of Guangxi, the Fangchenghekou ancient riverbed, and the Nanliu River mouth ancient riverbed. In the mid-Holocene, as sea level continued to rise, seawater continued to flow into the ancient Beilun River, forming an estuary. A layer of estuarine facies silty clay was accumulated, whose representative species included Hope Bug, Asian Hope Bug, flattened worms, American Flower Bug, Pacific roseworm, Trichophyton of the bulb, Bottle worm, wrinkled new single-angled species, Musaike, Xiao Kejie, and others, reflecting the estuarine sedimentary environment at that time. By the middle and late Holocene, after the formation of the Hekou estuary, due to the north and northwest sides being large areas of eroded and exploited hills, under the cutting effect of rivers and floods, a large amount of sediment was discharged into the river mouth bay, causing the estuary to be constantly filled and silted up. Meanwhile, the shore waves caused by southerly and southeasterly winds sorted and accumulated sediments in the shoals along the shore. Then, under the diversion of waves and the effect of the southeastward coastal current, the accumulation was distributed parallel to the coast, forming underwater sand dams. Therefore, in the estuary sedimentary layer, there is a sandy sediment layer piled up on top. After the underwater sand dam formed, under the effect of the shore waves it continuously strengthened and gradually emerged above the water surface to form a sandbar. Low-lying lagoons or shallow shoals were formed between it and the land. These lagoons or shoals, under the combined effects of river mud and sand, tides and waves, wind and wave forces, and artificial modification and seawall construction, gradually filled into marine accumulation plains and connected with sand embankments to form land, such as the coastal plain sand embankment landscape in the Zhushan area. The modern Beilun River carries sediment that accumulates at the estuary, and under the influence of tidal current trends, a sandbar forms near the river mouth. Due to the strong tidal force at the outer edge of the river mouth area, the action of rivers is weak, forming a dynamic geomorphic form with tidal sand ridges and tidal gullies side by side. In conclusion, the formation and evolution of various dynamic landforms in the Beilun River estuary area generally went through the stages from before the Holocene with subaerial weathering and erosion, to the early Holocene sea level rise forming an ancient river channel, to the mid-Holocene seawater intrusion forming an estuary, to the middle and late Holocene estuary infilling and formation of sand dams within the bay, and finally to the late Holocene gradual formation of marine accumulation plains, coastal sand embankments, Hekou sandbars, and tidal sand ridges.

Tidal control sand bodies are widely distributed in Marine strata and are favorable reservoirs. Analyzing the sedimentary and distribution characteristics of tidal control sand bodies can, on the one hand, provide a basis for the research of ancient tidal control sand bodies through the idea of discussing the past from the present, and offer a direction for the prediction of favorable sand bodies and the selection of exploration targets in oil and gas exploration and development. On the other hand, it can promote the research on the surface evolution and the dynamic mechanism of the sedimentary system in the tide-controlled coastal system, and enrich the modern research theory of tide-controlled sediment bodies. In addition, it also has certain reference significance for the full utilization of coastal zone sedimentary landscapes and the design of landscape protection projects. This thesis selects two typical tide-controlled bays, Moon Bay and Chaoyang Port in Jiaodong Peninsula, as the research areas. Based on ALOS DEM, Google Earth, Landsat images, regional geological survey, reconnaissance and unmanned aerial vehicle outcrop scanning, the topographic and geomorphic data, shoreline changes and elevation data of the research were obtained. Lithology, grain size, mineral and elemental data were obtained through grain size analysis, whole-rock X-ray diffraction and X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy analysis. Based on the above data, the sedimentary characteristics of two types of tidal control sediment bodies were analyzed, the sedimentary phases were classified, and the controlling factors influencing the development of tidal control sediment bodies were summarized. The research results show that Moon Bay is a tide-controlled semi-enclosed bay. The sedimentary facies are mainly divided into the suprtidal zone, intertidal zone and subtidal zone. The main sediment types are silty sandy mud and medium-fine sand. The typical sedimentary features mainly include tidal rhythm bedding, interlaced bedding and double clay bedding, etc. Chaoyang Port is a closed bay with a strong tide control barrier island and lagoon. The sedimentary facies mainly include ebb and flow deltas and tidal waterways. The main types of sediments are silt, medium-coarse sand and fine gravel. The typical sedimentary features mainly include tidal beams, tidal sand waves and composite sand dunes, etc. The geomorphic features of the tidal control coastal zone mainly include the superimposition of multiple tidal sand ridges and the development of various forms of tidal current channels between sand DAMS. The semi-enclosed bay tidal basin is widely distributed with large areas of tidal sand DAMS, tidal sand ridges and tidal flats and other landforms. Closed bays usually present a barrier island system that is short and wide and parallel to the coastline. In areas with large tidal capacity and at the mouth, large areas of ebb and flow trilobes and linear tidal sand DAMS are prone to develop. Most of the sand bodies in the study area have a thickness of 1-3 meters, a length of 50-350 meters, and a width of 20-280 meters. It is clear that the length of the tidal control sand bodies is positively correlated with the width, and the length is approximately positively correlated with the thickness. Both types of tidal control environments have the potential to form higher-level thick-layer tidal sand dam complexes, and tidal dunes are the most indicative of intense tidal action. In the research of controlling factors, it was found that suitable terrain and slope are beneficial to the formation of sand bodies. The source of materials controls the scale of the development of tidal sand bodies. Hydrodynamic control of the main morphology and lithological grain size distribution of sand bodies; Human activities such as water conservancy projects, land reclamation from the sea and aquaculture reclamation directly affect the characteristics of sediment sources, spatial distribution and migration trends. Finally, by analyzing the shoreline changes and sand dam development in the two study areas, the evolution process of tidal sand bodies was restored. In the established sedimentary model of tidal sand bodies, the favorable development positions and formation processes of tidal sand bodies were clearly defined.

Under the Qilihai formation lie Late Pleistocene fluvial and lacustrine deposits. Some boreholes reveal tidal channels and tidal current delta deposits, and rapidly developing tidal flat and salt marsh facies, with overlying coastal dune deposits. The sandbar was formed at approximately 7 cal kyr BP, then remained stable, and entered a period of rapid development at about 3.6 cal kyr BP. From 2.10 to 1.65 cal kyr BP, the lagoon filled rapidly and the scale of the sandbar expanded rapidly. After 616 cal yr BP, the beach sand rapidly accumulated towards the sandbar under the force of wind, forming coastal sand dunes. ~ 3.6 cal kyr BP. The climate in the Yanshan area has become dry and cold. The vegetation type has deteriorated from Quercus - Pinus - Artemisia to Pinus - Quercus - Artemisia. The reduction of precipitation and the degradation of vegetation jointly lead to the Luan River being more prone to course change. The eastward migration of the Luan River has increased the supply of material sources, providing a material basis for the development of the Qilihai sand dam. The strengthened East Asian winter monsoon during the Little Ice Age led to a drier and colder climate. The vegetation of the Qilihai sandbar deteriorated into a desert steppe type. The powerful wind blew the beach sand to the sandbar and directly buried the salt marshes behind it.

Beach conservation has been widely carried out abroad and has become a major measure to prevent coastal erosion. In recent years, China has begun to attach importance to beach maintenance, but it is relatively lacking in experience and theoretical guidance. Research mainly focuses on beach maintenance design and simulation, while there is less study on the evolution of beaches after maintenance, and there is a lack of understanding of whether beach maintenance develops as expected. This article mainly analyzes the changes in the surface sediments and the topography of the middle beach in Beidaihe after its maintenance, which can provide a reference for scientifically analyzing the effectiveness of beach maintenance and subsequent beach maintenance projects. Based on the maintenance project of the middle beach in Beidaihe, Qinhuangdao, this paper conducts experiments on the particle size of surface sediment samples at different periods during the maintenance of the middle beach to obtain sediment particle size parameters. Using the Gao-Collins particle size trend model, the characteristics of sediment particle size changes after beach maintenance and their influence on the maintained beach are studied. The RMAP software was used to process and map the beach profile data of different time periods monitored by the total station and depth sounder. Combined with the sediment transport calculation along the shore, the change characteristics of the nearshore topography and landforms before and after beach maintenance were analyzed. The results show that under the continuous action of waves and other dynamic forces, the sediments of the new beach after beach maintenance tend to be refined and have better sorting properties. The beach maintenance effect is good, and the net migration trend of sediments gradually changes from coastal transport to vertical transport. The construction and reconstruction of artificial headlands have played a positive role in maintaining the stability of the beach. The sand-throwing substances gradually mix with the original beach sediments and provide a certain source of materials for the adjacent areas. They have a significant impact on the characteristics and changes of sediments inside and outside the sand-throwing area in a short period of time, and this influence has spatio-temporal differences. With the passage of time, this influence gradually weakens. After the beach was filled with sand, the shoreline clearly advanced towards the sea. Subsequently, the shoreline continuously eroded and retreated, with the erosion being most significant during the initial stage of beach maintenance. After 18 months of beach maintenance, the total loss of sand filling on the beach was 43%. The average width of the beach in the sand-filling area has increased by approximately 15 meters, and that outside the sand-filling area has increased by about 11 meters. The rate of beach erosion has significantly slowed down, indicating that the beach is evolving towards a stable trend. The sediment transport along the coast is mainly influenced by the NNE-S waves, with the transport mainly from NE to SW. The total net sediment transport in a year is approximately 4.18×10^4 m³, forming a shoreline pattern with upstream erosion and downstream accumulation.

Beach conservation has become a major measure to prevent coastal erosion and has been widely carried out in Europe, America, Japan and other countries, with rich experience and theories. In recent years, it has gradually gained attention in China, but it is still in its infancy. Research mainly focuses on beach maintenance design and simulation, with relatively few studies on the evolution of beaches after maintenance. There is a lack of understanding of whether the maintained beaches develop towards the expected effects. This requires later monitoring and analysis of beach sediments, beach topography, hydrodynamic conditions, and ecology after beach maintenance. This article mainly analyzes the changes in the surface sediments and the topography of the West Beach of Beidaihe after its maintenance, in order to provide reference materials for the beach maintenance work. Through particle size screening experiments on surface sediment samples of Beidaihe West Beach before maintenance, at the beginning of maintenance, 3 months after maintenance and one year after maintenance, the particle size parameters were obtained. Combined with the Gao-Collins particle size trend model, the characteristics of sediment particle size changes after beach maintenance and their influence on the maintained beach were analyzed. The Regional Morphology Analysis Package (RMAP) was used to process and map the beach profile data monitored by the total station and depth sounder. Combined with the coastal sediment transport calculation, the change characteristics of the nearshore topography and geomorphology before and after beach maintenance were analyzed. The results showed that: (1) After being subjected to dynamic forces such as waves, the sediment of the newly maintained beach becomes coarser as a whole and has better sorting properties. One year later, the average particle size of the sediment decreased from 1.87φ to 1.4φ, and the sorting coefficient decreased from 0.97 to 0.81. The beach sediments are mainly displaced and strike-shifting substances. The net migration trend has changed from being dominated by offshore transportation in the early stage of conservation to being dominated by coastal transportation. Both indicate that some beach fill sands have been lost and provide a certain supply of sediments for the adjacent areas. Therefore, the sand-filling substances have a certain influence on the grain size characteristics of the sand-filling area and its upstream and downstream sediments at the beginning of maintenance. However, after a long time, the sand-filling substances gradually adapt to the dynamic conditions of the area, indicating that the selection of the sand-filling particle size is relatively appropriate. (2) After the sand filling on the beach was completed, the beach shoulder widened, and the shoreline advanced significantly towards the sea. The average single-width siltation was 38.3 cubic meters. After one year of evolution, the shoreline eroded and retreated significantly compared to the beginning of the sand filling. Particularly, the erosion of the shoreline section in the sand filling area was the most severe, while there was slight siltation outside the sand filling area. Overall, after six months of beach maintenance, the erosion rate gradually decreases, with a single-width erosion volume of approximately 0.9 to 5.4 cubic meters. After one year, about 9,925.5 cubic meters have been eroded by the fill sand, preserving approximately 88.7% of the total fill sand on the beach shoulder. The beach is evolving towards a stable trend. The impact of coastal sediment transport on beach conservation is greater than its lateral transport. Coastal transport is mainly from NE to SW, with an annual net sediment transport of approximately 1.2×10 ⁴ m ³ to 1.6×10 ⁴ m ³, forming a shoreline pattern where the fill area is significantly eroded and the downstream area is significantly silted up. The large-scale sand filling and maintenance behavior on the beach also has a significant impact on the nearshore topography and landforms, especially the morphological change characteristics of underwater sand DAMS, which also show certain seasonal variations. Compared with before the maintenance, the sand-filling area and its upstream and downstream beaches have both been widened to a certain extent, and the beach maintenance effect is good. (3) The auxiliary construction of hard engineering such as breakwaters and offshore submerges plays a positive role in reducing the energy of incoming waves from the open sea, intercepting coastal sand drift, promoting beach stability and reducing or preventing sediment loss.

The coastal sections of Jiangsu Province, except for the radiating sandbars, are generally in an eroded state due to the lack of sediment replenishment. In recent years, due to the protection of seawalls, the rate of coastline erosion and retreat has slowed down, but the extent of erosion has been expanding. This paper takes the silt coast in Jiangsu Province from the mouth of the Guanhe River in the north to Lusi in the south as the research object. Based on the research results of predecessors and combined with multiple cross-sectional topographic and sediment measurement data, it adopts research methods such as hydrology, sediment movement mechanics and dynamic sedigology to conduct a relatively in-depth and systematic study on the geomorphic evolution process, profile topographic features and sediment characteristics and distribution of the silt coast in Jiangsu The research conclusions are as follows: 1 On the north side of the abandoned Yellow River Delta: The sand spout outside the Guanhe River mouth is developing rapidly and approaching the bank. The cross-sectional topography consists of the bank slope - the west channel - the inner slope of the sand spout - the shallow sand spout. The elevation of the eastern section of the Zhongshan River Estuary generally decreases gently, and the elevation in summer is slightly lower than that in winter. There are still some remaining underwater delta terraces outside the Zhongshan River Port. Under the control of waves, the abandoned tip of the Yellow River and the sections north of it are prone to form sediment flows, which transport the sediment westward. 2. The sharp bank section of the abandoned Yellow River Estuary: After the Yellow River moved northward, the sediment supply was seriously insufficient, causing large-scale erosion of the underwater delta and continuous erosion and retreat of the shoreline. Currently, under the protection of coastal engineering, the retreat of the shoreline has stopped and turned into downward erosion. The sediments of the abandoned yellow riverbank section show the characteristics of gradually narrowing from the shore to the sea and gradually thickening from north to south. The section from Fenshun Estuary to Zhen Dong Sluice Bank is a severely eroded area. 3. Erosion process of the abandoned Yellow River Delta: The current velocity on the south side is generally higher than that on the north side. The nearshore current is a reciprocating flow parallel to the coast, and the waves on the north side are generally stronger than those on the south side. Near the shore, the slope on the north side is gentler than that on the south side, and the erosion intensity is less than that on the south side. In deep water areas, the current on the north side is relatively weak, and the erosion is also weaker than that on the south side. Near the mouth tip of the abandoned Yellow River, wave energy converges, and tidal currents participate in transportation, with the greatest intensity of erosion. 4. Southern wing of the abandoned Yellow River Delta: The rate of retreat and erosion has significantly slowed down. The underwater slope of the Bindan Port (SB5) section is relatively straight, indicating summer erosion and winter siltation. The tidal flats show an upward siltation and downward erosion, and sand DAMS are well developed. The coast shows the characteristics of a transition from siltation to erosion. The Sheyang Estuary (SB6) is demarcated by the estuary, with the north being the flood and the south being the siltation. In the sea area outside the estuary, the siltation occurs at the top and the flood at the bottom. Sediments have the characteristic of gradually thickening from the shore to the sea. 5. The Lusi Coast is located on the southern wing of the radial sandbar and is controlled by the Xiaomiao Flood Channel. The main cause of erosion is the reduction of sediment supplied to the coast, and the sediment lifted by waves is easily carried away by the current, resulting in the beach showing an erosion process. The Lusi Beach area shows the hidden erosion characteristics of small changes in the middle and high tide beaches and erosion in the low tide beaches. The annual variation of wind waves can be classified into three periods based on the frequency of the onshore wind: the high-frequency period (from October to January of the following year), the frequency transition period (February, March and September), and the low-frequency period (April to August).

Tidal channels are waterways that connect open sea surfaces with lagoons, bays or estuaries and other water bodies. They usually serve as good natural waterways, and their tidal waters can also provide good berthing conditions for ships. However, once the terrain of tidal channels changes, it may seriously affect navigation and water exchange. Therefore, the research on tidal channels is of great significance in the maintenance of waterways and the ecological environment protection of lagoons Noodles have significant value. Under the influence of various driving forces and natural conditions, the topography of tidal channels exhibits complex evolution patterns. However, existing studies usually fail to take into account the combined effects of multiple driving forces. Especially during extreme weather processes such as typhoons, the short-term drastic changes caused by overtopping water flow erosion in tidal channels still lack a reasonable description. Based on the proposed overhead water flow scouring algorithm, this paper establishes a three-dimensional unstructured grid mathematical model for the terrain evolution of tidal channels that can reasonably describe the coupling effects of nearshore waves, water flow and sediment. The established model is used to simulate and study the balanced cross-sectional area of tidal channels. Further, taking the tidal channel at the mouth of Wanquan River in Hainan as an example, the simulation reveals the terrain of tidal channels The intense evolution mechanism of shapes under the influence of extreme weather processes. The main research contents and conclusions of the paper are as follows: (1) Aiming at the problem that the existing phase average model cannot reasonably calculate the sediment transport in the area above the average water surface, a set of overtopping water flow sediment transport algorithm has been developed in the three-dimensional wave-flow-sediment motion coupling model. This algorithm is divided into two situations based on different water levels: wave-crossing sediment transport and submerged sediment transport. Among them, the wave-crossing sediment transport algorithm is further classified into three types: the climbing zone, the top crossing zone, and the back slope zone. The submerged sediment transport algorithm calculates the sediment transport rate by using different parameter values on the sea-facing side and the shore side of the sand dam's top vertex respectively. (2) The three-dimensional model of the terrain evolution of the tidal channel established was verified respectively by using the on-site observation data of sediment transport along the coast, the hydrodynamic and topographic evolution experiments of the tidal channel, and the overhead water flow scouring experiments. The results show that the model established in this paper can well simulate the distribution of coastal current and sediment transport along the coast, the hydrodynamic characteristics and topographic evolution laws of the tidal channel under different conditions, as well as the passage The process of erosion and siltation development of terrain profiles caused by wave erosion and inundation erosion. (3) An ideal generalized tidal channel terrain was constructed, and the evolution process of the tidal channel under different tidal range, wave height and lagoon area was simulated. The simulation results show that the cross-sectional area increases with the increase of tidal range, decreases with the increase of wave height causing the increase of sediment transport along the shore, and increases with the increase of lagoon area but gradually tends to be constant. By combining the dimensional analysis method with simulation data, a new empirical formula for calculating the balanced cross-sectional area of tidal channels was proposed. This formula has a good consistency with the on-site measured data of tidal channels in different regions. (4) Taking the Wanquan River Estuary as an example, the evolution of the tidal channel terrain during typhoon processes was studied. By analyzing various satellite remote sensing image data in recent years, it was found that the tidal channel terrain at the Wanquan River Estuary shows two changing trends: when the typhoon process is strong, the shape of the tidal channel usually undergoes more drastic changes, with the width of the estuary significantly increasing. In extreme cases, it even causes a collapse in the middle of the Yudai Beach Break, forming dual channels; After the storm process's effect ends, the tidal channel and its surrounding shoreline are mainly affected by sediment transport along the shore, resulting in siltation and a slow narrowing of the opening width. (5) A coupled assimilation model of atmosphere, ocean and waves was established to simulate the wind field process and hydrodynamic process of Typhoon Haiyan. A mathematical model of the terrain evolution of the tidal channel at the Wanquan estuary based on the overpass water flow sediment transport algorithm was nested to simulate the evolution process of the tidal channel at the Wanquan Estuary under the influence of Typhoon Haiyan, revealing the evolution mechanism. The simulation results show that the typhoon process causes a relatively significant water level rise in the Wanquan River Estuary sea area. The middle part of the Yudai Beach is affected by overwave sediment transport and inundation sediment transport, resulting in a breach. Under the subsequent flood action, the breach widens and deepens, forming a double opening of the tidal channel.

Lagoon tidal channels are a common landform on sandy coasts. Among them, lagoons usually have good protective conditions and are excellent places for port sites and ships to take shelter from the wind in storm conditions. Tidal channels are natural waterways that connect lagoons with the open sea. Due to the influence of factors such as tidal currents, waves, and nearshore wave currents, the sediment movement in the lagoon tidal channels is often more active, and the terrain changes are also more frequent. The stability of lagoon tidal channels is closely related to port construction, shipping, and nearshore ecosystems. Therefore, research on their stability has always been an important topic in the field of coastal engineering. The closure of tidal channels caused by sediment transport along the coast is an important manifestation of its instability and has a significant negative impact on human production. This paper adopts a process-based mathematical model to study the topographic evolution of lagoon tidal channels under different wave and tidal current conditions, and explores the laws of topographic evolution when the coastal sediment transport caused by wave-induced coastal currents and tidal sediment transport act together. The main work of this paper is as follows :(1) The oblique incident waves in the nearshore area break up to form coastal currents, and the coastal sediment transport they cause is an important factor affecting the topographic evolution of the lagoon tidal channel. Whether the calculation of coastal current and coastal sediment transport is reasonable mainly depends on the simulation results of the nearshore wave field. To better simulate the propagation of waves in nearshore areas, this paper introduces the wave breaking index of Yoshitaka Koda into the SWAN model. Based on A large amount of on-site and laboratory observation data, the expression of coefficient a in the wave-breaking index formula was proposed, and relatively independent observation data were collected to verify the improved model. The simulation results have good consistency with the measured data. (2) Observational data from the object model experiment on the terrain evolution of the Keta Lagoon tidal channel conducted by the Delft Hydrodynamic Laboratory were collected. The experimental process was simulated by applying the mathematical model of terrain evolution. The results show that the model can reasonably describe the terrain evolution law of the lagoon tidal channel under the combined action of tidal current and waves. (3) The topographic evolution laws of the ideal lagoon tidal channels under different wave heights and tide levels were studied. The results show that the coastal sediment transport caused by the nearshore wave current is the main factor leading to the formation of the shallow beach at the entrance of the tidal channel. The higher the wave height, the more obvious the development of the shallow beach of the barrier sand at the entrance of the tidal channel. Tidal current is the main factor that inhibits the formation of shallows at the mouth and maintains the unobstructed flow of tidal channels. The greater the tidal range, the less obvious the development of shallows. When the tidal force is weak and the wave conditions are significant, the openings of the lagoon tidal channels tend to close.

The Laolonggou tidal channel system in the Caofeidian sea area mainly consists of three geomorphic units: offshore sand dams, lagoons, and deep troughs of the Koumen channel. The existence of the sandbar creates a dual shoreline in this sea area and separates the lagoon shoal water on the inner side of the sandbar from the open sea, resulting in dynamic conditions that are significantly different from the landform features. The Laolonggou Lagoon is connected to the open sea by two deep channels, the east and the west. Among them, the water area of the west branch channel widens rapidly, causing the low-tide water flow to spread, and the sediment keeps silting down, developing large-scale barrier sand shoals. (2) The Laolonggou tidal channel system is the result of the long-term transformation of the abandoned delta of the ancient Luan River. Due to the significant amount of sediment discharged from the Luan River into the sea in recent decades, the supply of sediment along the coast is insufficient. The sea area outside the sand dams such as Dongkengtuo is generally in a state of erosion and scouring, and the contour lines are constantly receding towards the land. Among them, from 1996 to 2006 the average seabed depth increased by approximately 0.5 to 2.0 meters. Some of the eroded sediment is deposited at the tail of Dongkengtuo under the combined action of coastal current and rising current, and within 10 years it has silted up by approximately 1,200 meters. Due to the insufficient supply of sediment along the coast, less sediment enters the Laolonggou tidal channel, which is a deep trough basically showing a trend of erosion. The sediment washed down from the deep trough mainly settles in the sand area at the entrance gate. (3) The long-term oscillation of the deep groove at the west branch of Laolonggou is closely related to the evolution of Dongkengtuo. The scouring development of the middle trough is mainly in the southwest of Dongkengtuo. The siltation at the tail end presses on the deep trough at the entrance of Laolonggou, causing the cape effect to increase and the tidal current force to strengthen. The swinging phenomenon of the west trough mainly develops in conjunction with that of the east trough, and it is related to the extrusion effect produced. Due to the insufficient supply of sediment in this sea area in recent years, the siltation rate of the deep trough from the southwest tail end of Dongkengtuo to the west branch of Laolonggou has gradually slowed down, so both the development speed of the east trough and the swing speed of the west trough have significantly decreased, from 30 to 80 m/a between 1996 and 2006 to the current level of about 5 to 10 meters per year.

After discussing the characteristics of the Shuidong tidal and tidal channel system and its coastal sediment transport mode, the following points can be recognized. The Shuidong Ke Lake tidal stream channel system is very typical. The tide flows out of its mouth on the delta, transporting sludge and sand, but the quantity is not large. The thickness of the fine sand on the surface that is easy to be lifted is minimal. Moreover, the wave energy level is low, and the average wave height is small. Under normal circumstances, sudden siltation does not occur. During a storm surge, the tide level can rise significantly, and even if sediment is piled up on the waterway, the strong low tide after the storm has the power to carry it away, increasing flow velocity and enhancing scouring capacity. For a tidal channel in a stable equilibrium state, there is generally no need to worry about sediment accumulation. The extension and movement of the impulse sand dam are important ways for sediment transport on the delta during the eastward ebb tide, contributing to the construction of waterways, but they pose certain threats. Therefore, appropriate engineering measures should be taken for protection. The mud and sand entering from the rising tide ditches on both sides of the main passage will accumulate along the banks, which is disadvantageous for the construction of docks and ports. Some protective measures must be in place, but the tidal channel must not be blocked, otherwise the system will absorb too much moisture and undergo transformations with the tide phase, potentially leading to worse consequences.

The coastal zone is a sensitive area for environmental changes. Under the influence of both natural and human forces, the resource and environmental problems in the coastal zone have become increasingly prominent. Understanding the adaptability of the coastal zone system and its openness in response to environmental changes is an important topic in coastal geomorphology research. Tidal channels are an important subsystem of the coastal zone system and are particularly sensitive to environmental changes. From the perspective of dynamic geomorphic systems, this paper studies the physical processes of interaction and adaptation among the various units of the tidal channel system in maintaining system stability, analyzes the impact of environmental changes on the system and the responses of each unit of the system to environmental changes, and proposes the principles, methods and approaches for the sustainable utilization of tidal channel resources. Firstly, the internal characteristics of the tidal channel system were studied. Through the analysis of the characteristics of the interaction between the morphology and dynamics of tidal channels, as well as the geomorphic structure and hydrodynamic and sediment transport features, the close connection of the mutual influence and interaction among the various units of the system is discussed, and the adaptability of the tidal channel system to maintain stability is analyzed. Based on the research of modern dynamic effects and geomorphic morphology combinations, the dynamic geomorphological classification of tidal inlets was carried out. The intertidal channel between some tidal ridges in the radiating sandbar area off the shore of Jiangsu Province, which has the characteristics of the interaction between the geomorphic morphology and dynamic forces of tidal channels, is regarded as a special type of tidal channel. The geomorphic basis, dynamic environment and dynamic trend of its development are studied. In the research on the openness characteristics of the system, the environmental response of the tidal channel system was studied from the aspect of the connection between the system and the external environment. By means of coastal dynamic geomorphology, mathematical models and physical models respectively, case studies were conducted on sanddam lagoon type, gully (estuary bay) type and inundation type tidal channels, and the modern dynamic geomorphology processes and responses to environmental changes of various types of tidal channels were specifically analyzed. Based on the research of the internal and open characteristics of the system, the sustainable utilization of tidal channel resources was studied. The characteristics of tidal channel resources and the main problems existing in their sustainable utilization were summarized. Through the analysis of resource carrying capacity, the principles for utilizing tidal channel resources, the methods for evaluating the current utilization status and diagnosing obstacles to sustainable utilization, as well as the approaches to enhancing the level of sustainable resource utilization are proposed. Restore

The sandbar moistening lake-type tidal Han Dao along the South China coast is mainly distributed at the entrance of the Han Dao in the small tidal range of the Kunwan coast section. There is a deep slot at the entrance of this landscape unit, including the low tide delta and rising tide delta. The shape and form characteristics of the inwardly curved sand mouth and the seashore dynamics and sediment conditions are related to the migration of the Han Dao entrance. The main water channel moves at low tide, and the direction of the thrust dam and the edge dam of the waterway, along with shore relocation and shore consolidation, form the main pattern of the evolution of the Han Dao Kou Men landform and have periodic characteristics. The Tear Lake Bay is large-scale; reclamation blocks the sea, or rivers flowing into the harbor bay are diverted directly to the sea. The activity of these interventions could lead to sudden changes at the Han Dao Gate, deteriorating navigation conditions and creating the need for rectification.

Based on the existing research results on the channel improvement of the Minjiang River estuary, more than 130 surface sediment samples were collected at the estuary. Based on the analysis of sedimentary structure, types of sand DAMS at the estuary and hydrodynamic conditions, the distribution law and sedimentation mechanism of sediments at the Minjiang River estuary were discussed. The research results show that 1) the estuary of the Minjiang River can be divided into three sedimentary areas: sandy, mixed and muddy, which respectively represent the delta leading edge, the leading edge slope and the pre-delta sedimentary environment; 2) The diameters and tidal current intensities of each branch at the mouth of the Minjiang River are different, and there are significant differences in the sedimentation process at the mouth. Runoff plays a leading role in the development of the river and stone waterways, and tidal currents are the main factor shaping the plum blossom waterways. 3) The types of estuarine sand DAMS are restricted by the diffusion form of the outflowing water. There are various types of estuarine sand DAMS at the mouth of the Minjiang River. The estuarine sand dam of the Chuanshi Waterway is of the underwater causeway type, and an underwater causeway type sand dam has developed on the north side of the Wuzhu Waterway. The Yandou Waterway is a damage-blocking sand type estuarine sand dam, and the Meihua Waterway is a tidal ridge type estuarine sand dam.

Qinglan Kehu 'Shaba' tidal channel system has a relatively strong tidal current effect, and the intensity of wave action is moderate. The Wenjiao River and Wenchang River discharge over ten thousand tons of sediment every year, which is mainly deposited in Bamen Bay and the northern section of the tidal channel. The sedimentation rate is high, and the substance consists of fine sand, silt, and clay. The sediments in the southern section of the tidal channel and the estuary area come from the erosion of marine terraces, modern coastal erosion, and early land sand supply frameworks. They are under the influence of tides and waves. There is a vertical axis circular transfer of sediment on each of the west sides of the system. The transferred substances are mainly fine sand, medium, and coarse sand from the shallow shoals of the inner and outer gates and the deep troughs of the tidal current at the entrance. Fine sand mostly belongs to the seabed residual sedimentation. The continuous distribution characteristics of sediment reflect a close relationship between deep trough scouring and shallow beach accumulation. East of the entrance of Kehu Lake, the formation time of the sand ridges in Xilanwan is relatively recent. The formation of sand ridges along the eastern bay and coral reefs, and the expansion of the plateau, is closely related to wave convergence failure. The western sand ridges, which are composed of biological debris, are developed in Gaolong Bay. Their formation is related to the divergence of waves and the accumulation of sediment. The substances consist of quartz and biodebris.

The migration of the eastern shoreline of Laizhou Bay and the erosion and siltation of the underwater shoreline slope have significant nonlinear characteristics, presenting the feature of alternating erosion and siltation. From 1959 to 2013, the rate of shoreline erosion fluctuated, first decreasing and then increasing with 1985 as the boundary. The shoreline changes of each cape and bay are not consistent with the overall situation. Except for the Sanshan Island - Diaolongzui shoreline section which has been in a state of siltation or equilibrium, the shoreline evolution of the other three headlands and bays on a ten-year scale shows a spatial pattern of alternating erosion and siltation. From 1959 to 1985, the shoreline advanced towards the sea as a whole. Meanwhile, the water depths at the 2m,5m and 10m isobars of the underwater shoreline all silted up and became shallower. The silted area and the amount of sediment were 1.87×10^8 m2 and 1.15×10^8 m3 respectively. From 1985 to 2006, the shoreline as a whole eroded and retreated. The water depth at the 2-meter contour line increased, remained basically unchanged at the 5-meter contour line, and the 10-meter contour line became shallower due to siltation. The siltation area was only 5.58×10^7 m2, and the amount of silted sediment was 8% of the previous period. Continuous underwater erosion bands have appeared on the outer side of most sections of the coastline. The sedimentary type of the buried samples on the eastern coast of Laizhou Bay belongs to medium and coarse sand, with a medium to good degree of sorting, mainly positive deviation and extremely positive deviation. 66.67% originated from shallow sea sediments. The current exposure of shallow sea and ancient beach sediments indicates that this section of the shore has been in a state of erosion and regression recently. The surface sediments of 148 beaches mainly belong to coarse sand, medium sand and extremely coarse sand, and the sorting degree is mainly good and medium. The average particle size of the profile located near the boundary river estuary is the coarsest and gradually decreases downstream. The hydrodynamic numerical simulation results show that as the amount of sediment flowing into the sea from rivers decreases, the overall erosion effect becomes increasingly significant, the range continuously expands, and the intensity continuously increases. Different sections of the bank present different erosion and siltation states. The erosion bands are mainly distributed between the 2-5m isobars, and the erosion is severe near the nearshore estuary. However, except for a few locations such as Jiehekou, Wanghekou and the southwest of Diaolongzui where strong erosion occurred, the areas shallower than the 2-meter isodepth line are all in a state of balance between erosion and siltation or slight siltation. Under the control of coastal boundary conditions and the frequent occurrence of storm surges, the erosion and siltation changes of the coastal landform in this section are unstable at least on a 60-year scale. Under the combined effect of the geomorphic effects of human activities such as the exchange of sediment between beaches and underwater sand DAMS, the transformation from headlands and bays to straight sandy shores, as well as the sand interception by reservoirs, factory aquaculture in the supramtide zone, and large-scale coastal engineering, there have been at least five alternations of shoreline advance and retreat and the rate of erosion and siltation in the past 60 years. On average, a transformation occurs every ten years, and it is not synchronized with different sections of the shoreline.
